item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it is owned by Omega Artworks (as indicated here), with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: not sure
item.content:encoded:
I am iffy about this… buuuuut +1, since you didn't do the urban legend of the rake instead of coming up with this idea.

over all the article felt a little lacking to me, I cant put my finger on it thou. I will get back to you when I can find out what it is.

actually I just cant upvote for that no vote till its a little brushed up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They might have to check his passport again due to a suspiciously recurrent computer glitch. Same taking-aside-out-of-sight and much much less personal media coverage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been here for like eight months, lurking for two more before that, and I honestly haven't written a damn thing site-worthy yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahhh, missed that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was waiting for someone to talk about this, I've been worried about this point since posting, but no one mentioned it so I figured it wasn't a problem.

Thanks, I'll get on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MTF Delta-23 is to apprehend SCP-1847 upon landing under the premise of terrorist activity. SCP-1847 is then to be transferred to Site-117.

They'd have to do a little more cover-up here, otherwise people will notice that the same terrorist is being arrested over and over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Every upvote matters to me.

I am a noob myself; only been here eight weeks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I myself chuckled at that. I only just noticed the title, and I chuckled some more. I think this is a pretty good skip, and you get my upvote, if it means anything to you, from such a n00b.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was worried the MTF name was going to draw massive eye-rolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well it looks like we got a real ladykiller on our hands. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm.

Should I make it "England, the United Kingdom" or leave it as "England, United Kingdom"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am considering expanding on this article at some point. I feel that some of the content here is not sufficiently expressed. In particular, I'd like to give more on the jewels, the thing in the dagger, and whatever the fuck is going on in the last addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really? I've never heard of that before.

Of course, I'm American, so that might have something to do with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I felt like describing SCP-1847's appearance and its effects thoroughly was important, since the Foundation has to go identify this guy and capture him several times a year. To be fair, I don't think I really went too in-depth on describing him anyway, and did my best to avoid using his name or a personal pronoun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, a rake is a British literary character type, so it is appropriate, but I'm not going to pretend like I wasn't having fun with the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, when I saw the name on the lists page I assumed it was going to be that awful "The Rake" rip off that someone was talking about on the forums a few weeks ago.

Pleasantly surprised. But I do wonder: is the name is intentional or not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He has actually already done this. He was originally using Great Britain, which refers to England, Scotland and Wales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are definitely playing with fire here. If not for that first footnote, I'd have downvoted right there and left the tab. The ultimate story is, I think, worth an upvote, but given that the first part of this article is "Look how pretty my dude is", edging up on an X-Man entry, something might be said for making the dagger the focus of the article, along with the Foundation's efforts to recover it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding. I saw "England" and my immediate reaction was, "All right, does he really mean England, or the UK?" Would help clear that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Manifestations of SCP-1847 is to be housed in a humanoid containment cell

are to be housed.

SCP-1847 is to remain under video and audio surveillance constantly

Constant video and audio serveillance, although I believe both are correct.

Also, maybe elaborating slightly on how it disappears? Is there a puff of smoke? Is it instantaneous or does it take a few seconds or minutes? Does it fade through the floor?

I rather enjoyed the MCD reference, nicely done.

A positive-neutral upvote from me. This could definitely turn out to be something amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree about the MC&D bit because it doesn't kind of fit how that group works: they sell things to people that are interested in them. They don't care about the safety of their customers, or honesty. They care about doing business, and if something goes wrong, about making sure word doesn't spread.

I like to think that the scip wasn't entirely the result of the dagger alone. Maybe the dagger originally just had some minor side effect that the customer complained about. Maybe it only made its user beautiful under very specific circumstances, or maybe it only produced jewels that it had obtained from someone else's possession.

And maybe MC&D decided complaints were bad for business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "I swear, officer! He just became England!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one cant be contained. And this is a specific person. Who stays the same gender.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of has an X-files feel to it, in a good way. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah England! For that good sir, you have earned yourself a cookie and a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-056, 'A Beautiful Person'. We've got that already, and we contain it fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After sleeping on it, I realize it's actually less interesting than a non-anomalous serial-killer. This one reappears at predictable places, in an environment where he can be easily apprehended. A mundane killer would in fact be much more fun harder to track down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You got the upvote.

A man so handsome that he can't be contained is pretty awesome. Even if he isn't really handsome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh boy, an involuntary, time-trapped, compulsive incubus! Take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that's what Roget was saying too, so…I suppose I can change this to Euclid; it's definitely hostile and hard to contain, but it doesn't have the destructive quality necessary for Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, so tenative upvote, but this definitely isn't keter.

Honestly, if it stays keter, I'm likely yo change to a downvote. This is euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, Dr. Bright upvoted this? What did I do right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You are mistaken.

An ornate dagger with a ruby-encrusted, golden hilt (SCP-1847-1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I mistaken, or do you never actually describe 1847-1 within the actual Description?

The writing is decent, but I find the content mediocre, unfortunately. Magical rapist serial killer manifestations: Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We could debate the merits of using a 5 year old article to judge modern containment standards, but that would be getting off-topic. Let's stop this here.

He changed the object class to Euclid, so this is moot anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, but look at 682. It doesn't exactly kill very many people when loose. It's basically just an angry, indestructible tiger. And this thing is way harder to contain than 682.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh crap. I think should fix the "all female individuals" part. At the risk of losing your upvote, I'd like to clarify that he does not actually attract lesbians.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the containment could be fixed up a bit. If you could go on about how he disappears, what does video surveillance show? A bright flash or static and then nothing. Can cameras even capture his image? He is said to be handsome.

As for the plane fiasco, maybe a random glitch in the system introduces his name and he gets dropped off by an unmarked taxi.

Its a great SCP without a doubt. But I feel like some things are missing. Upvote for his attracting lesbians.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'androphilic individuals that identify as female' could be a simply fix for technical accuracy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, for this to be Keter he'd have to kill people on the level of SCP-755 which he doesn't really do. It's about five people dying on occasion, which isn't really enough to make it Keter(at least for me)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it makes more sense to keep it as is, because it doesn't apply to all androphilic individuals, just those that identify as "female" (homosexual and bisexual men are unaffected, as are female-to-male transgender individuals who are attracted to men).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Derp. You're right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's androphilic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you figure this isn't Keter? It's uncontained, and kills a pretty large number of people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can use the word gynephilic to simplify things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted for three reasons.

1. This is in no way Keter.

2. The MC&D connection is out of character for that group and feels really tacked on.

3. This effect still does nothing to interest me. The interview comes close, but it just isn't enough.

I did appreciate the attempt at storytelling and see what you were trying to do with this, but it's just mediocre all around. I'd probably neutral vote if you fixed the first two issues, and upvote if you could spice up the effect to the point where the third issue is overcome. I don't know what to suggest to make it less dull though, just that it needs more. Something needs to just go completely off the rails here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, he's probably never been England either unless he's more anomalous than previously thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay I've fixed up the little things; thanks for the suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just 'England'. The first time you use it you should say 'England, United Kingdom'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is extremely good, but I'm not entirely sure if I want to change to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm holding off for now. Not sure if I like it or not… yet. It's well written, but does not feel very original to me.

Not sure about the phonecall addendum. If no voice or signal is detectable, how do we know he's even contacting someone and not just pretending to talk on his cell (I do that all the time; it makes me very attractive)

A typo: '' I have never been England before'' (I guess TO England).

I don't like the sentence ''will increase at a linear rate'' for something that can't be really measured quantitatively. I suggest just ''increase constantly''.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just England, not Scotland, Ireland, or Wales. Which term would be best used in this case?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My own mother actually pointed this out during reading. I'll edit it to heterosexual and bisexual women (of all ages), and transgendered (from male to female).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This dude is anomalously attractive to women?

Does that include lesbians? Transgendered individuals (either flavor)? Post-menopausal women?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The dagger feels slightly off for MC&D. I get that it's giving someone a chance to live out some weird fantasy, but that and the murdering should be spelled out in the note somehow.

Also, minor nitpick. Unless you're excluding Northern Ireland while including the rest of the UK, you should use 'United Kingdom', though I'm mostly saying this because I think changing it to solely England might help with the Victorian vibes MC&D has always given me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amusing pun in the title. This is decent, though the time-loopy bit seems a bit off. An item with this much in the way of side-effects seems like something MC&D would have to warn its buyer of.

Thought for how to patch the logical flaws: It's a picture of Dorian Gray deal. There's a real Mr. Whitword floating around, in possession of the original dagger, and no idea any of this is going on. He is, however, unaging. Every year, at Christmastime, a copy of him (aged to Mr. Whitword's "true" age) appears on a plane headed in to England, and seduces and kills some women. At the end of the year, it vanishes, and necklaces and jewelry belonging to the women killed appear wrapped around the original dagger. If the Foundation stops the copy, Mr. Whitword ages normally that year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, this is pretty cool. Good storytelling. I just know you're going to get complaints about the dude's effects on lady folk, but it ties in well with the SCP and isn't given undue weight, so I fail to see any issue with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't get the picture to work right so I'm just going to fix it later.
